judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports correctly and thoroughly address all three expectations with equivalent substance; Report 2 adds extra rigor (explicit FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY discussion, a plausible SET parameter bug) but both are comparable in meeting the stated expectations.

report_1.evidence: In Postgres, table owners (and superusers) **bypass row-level security policies by default**, regardless of `ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` and the `tenant_isolation` policies defined on `clients` and `appointments`... So every request to `GET /clients`... executes `SELECT id, tenant_id, name FROM clients` as `cedar_owner`. Because `cedar_owner` owns the table, the RLS policy is silently never evaluated

report_2.evidence: row-level security policies are **never applied to the table owner** (and never to superusers, regardless of anything else) unless the table has `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` set... `0001_init.sql` never issues `ALTER TABLE clients FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY`, and even if it did, `cedar_owner` is a superuser, so RLS would still be bypassed.

report_1.evidence: `cedar_owner` (schema-owning role) used **only** by `scripts/migrate.sh` / migration tooling, never by the running application... `cedar_app` (the existing least-privilege role from `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`) used for **all** application runtime traffic — this is what `DATABASE_URL` (and hence `backend/app/db.py`'s engine) should point at.

report_2.evidence: Point the application's runtime `DATABASE_URL` at the dedicated `cedar_app` role (created by `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`), not `cedar_owner`. `cedar_owner`/the Postgres init superuser should be reserved for running migrations/DDL only, never for serving application traffic.

report_1.evidence: `cedar_app` role is **not** part of that image bootstrap and is not in `db/migrations/`; it lives only in `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`, which both `README.md:6-9` and `docs/local-setup.md:6` explicitly call a **one-time, run-by-hand** step, and `reset-local-db.sh` never invokes it.

report_2.evidence: `create_app_role.sql` itself says in its header comment: "Not part of automatic database initialization; run by hand on first setup."... After reset, only `cedar_owner` exists again; `cedar_app` was never re-created because `reset-local-db.sh` only calls `migrate.sh`, not `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`.
